section: Chapter 3: Nazism and the Rise of Hitler

questions.question: What were the main problems faced by the Weimar Republic after WWI?
questions.correct_answer: The humiliating Treaty of Versailles, hyperinflation, and political instability.
questions.explanation: The Republic was forced to accept the 'War Guilt' clause and pay massive reparations, which led to the total collapse of the German mark.

questions.question: Describe the impact of the Great Depression on Germany.
questions.correct_answer: Industrial production fell by 40%, unemployment reached 6 million, and people lost confidence in democratic institutions.
questions.explanation: This economic crisis created the perfect environment for Hitler and the Nazi party to gain popular support.

questions.question: What was the 'Enabling Act' of 1933?
questions.correct_answer: An act that gave Hitler all powers to sideline Parliament and rule by decree.
questions.explanation: This act established a legal dictatorship in Germany and banned all political parties except the Nazi party.

questions.question: What was the 'Gestapo'?
questions.correct_answer: The Secret State Police of the Nazi regime.
questions.explanation: Along with the SS and the SD, they were given extra-constitutional powers to detain people in concentration camps without trial.

questions.question: What did Hitler mean by 'Lebensraum'?
questions.correct_answer: Living space: the belief that Germany needed to acquire new territories for settlement.
questions.explanation: This geopolitical concept was used to justify the invasion of Eastern Europe to enhance the resources and power of the German nation.

questions.question: Who were considered 'undesirables' in Nazi Germany?
questions.correct_answer: Jews, Gypsies, Blacks, Russians, Poles, and the physically/mentally disabled.
questions.explanation: The Nazis wanted a society of 'pure and healthy Nordic Aryans' and sought to eliminate anyone who didn't fit this biological ideal.

questions.question: What was the 'Final Solution'?
questions.correct_answer: The Nazi plan for the systematic mass murder of Jews.
questions.explanation: This was the state-sponsored mass killing carried out in gas chambers, primarily in death camps like Auschwitz.

questions.question: What role were women expected to play in Nazi society?
questions.correct_answer: To be good mothers, rear 'pure-blooded' Aryan children, and maintain the home.
questions.explanation: Hitler famously stated that in his state, 'the mother is the most important citizen,' but women were expected to stay away from politics.

questions.question: What was 'Jungvolk'?
questions.correct_answer: A Nazi youth organization for boys below the age of 14.
questions.explanation: At age 14, boys joined the Hitler Youth to receive vigorous physical and ideological training in war and worship of Hitler.

questions.question: What was the 'Holocaust' and how did the world learn about it?
questions.correct_answer: The systematic murder of 6 million Jews; information spread through diaries and testimonies of survivors after the war.
questions.explanation: As the war ended, the world became aware of the horrors committed in the ghettos and death camps.
questions.source_section: Ordinary People and the Crimes Against Humanity

notes.title: Hyperinflation
notes.content: In 1923, the value of the German Mark fell so low that people needed cartloads of currency to buy a single loaf of bread.

notes.title: Nazi Ideology
notes.content: Based on a racial hierarchy (Aryans at the top, Jews at the bottom) and Social Darwinism, believing that the strongest race would survive.

notes.title: Art of Propaganda
notes.content: The Nazis used language and media (films, posters, radio) to chillling effect, never using words like 'kill' or 'murder,' instead using terms like 'special treatment'.

facts.fact: Hitler became Chancellor of Germany on 30 January 1933.
Germany invaded Poland in September 1939, which started World War II.
The 'Tripartite Pact' was signed in 1940 between Germany, Italy, and Japan.
Auschwitz was one of the most notorious death camps used for mass executions in gas chambers.
